A block of mass 3 kg compresses a spring (k = 600 N/m) by 0.1 m and is released on a horizontal surface with μk = 0.25. How far does it travel before stopping? (g = 10 m/s²)

A man of mass 70 kg stands at the centre of a rotating platform with I_platform = 200 kg·m² spinning at 2 rad/s. He walks to the edge (R = 2 m). What is the new angular velocity? (treat man as point mass)
A particle has position x = 5 sin(2πt) metres. What is the maximum acceleration?
A rotating platform of mass 100 kg and radius 2 m (I = ½MR²) is rotating at 3 rad/s. A 50 kg person stands at the rim. The person walks to the centre. What is the new angular velocity?
A 0.2 kg mass attached to a spring (k = 80 N/m) is displaced 0.05 m from equilibrium and released. What is the maximum KE during oscillation?
What is the magnitude of the resultant of three vectors: A = 3i, B = 4j, C = 12k?
Two pendulums have lengths in the ratio 4:1. What is the ratio of their periods?
A solid cylinder (I = ½MR²) rolls without slipping down an incline of height 2 m. What is its speed at the bottom? (g = 10 m/s²)
A 1000 kg car brakes from 30 m/s to rest. The braking distance is 45 m. What was the braking force?
A 2 kg mass oscillates on a spring (k = 50 N/m). At t = 0, x = 0.1 m and v = 0. What is the displacement at t = π/5 s?

A particle undergoes circular motion with radius 4 m. Its speed at angle θ = 0 is 3 m/s with tangential acceleration aₜ = 2 m/s². What is the total acceleration when θ = π/2 (after traveling a quarter circle)?
A 6 kg mass is dropped from 20 m onto a spring (k = 12,000 N/m). What is the maximum compression? (g = 10 m/s²; neglect mass of spring)
Kepler's third law states T² ∝ r³ for planets orbiting the Sun. Earth has r = 1 AU, T = 1 yr. A planet at r = 4 AU has period:
A wheel of radius 0.5 m rolls without slipping on a flat surface at 4 m/s. What is the speed of the point at the top of the wheel?
A 10 kg crate is pushed by a horizontal force F along a surface (μk = 0.3) for 5 m, reaching a final speed of 4 m/s from rest. What is F? (g = 10 m/s²)
Two forces of 5 N and 12 N act at right angles. What is the magnitude of their resultant?
A particle undergoes two successive displacements: d₁ = 3i + 4j m and d₂ = −i + 3j − 2k m. What is the magnitude of the total displacement?
A force F = 10 N acts at an angle of 60° to a displacement of 5 m. What is the work done?
The period of a satellite orbiting Earth at radius r from the centre is T = 2π√(r³/GM). What does the period become when r is tripled?

A uniform square plate of side a and mass M is free to rotate about one edge. It is held horizontal and released. What is the angular acceleration initially? (g = 10 m/s², I_edge = 4Ma²/3 for a square)
Write the dimensions of a/b in the relation P = (a − t²) / bx, where P is pressure, x is distance, and t is time.
An object is dropped from a height and takes 4 s to reach the ground. From what height was it dropped? (g = 10 m/s²)
Two bodies of masses 2 kg and 3 kg moving in the same direction at 6 m/s and 4 m/s respectively. They collide and the 2 kg body moves at 3 m/s after collision. What is the velocity of the 3 kg body?
A heat engine absorbs 1200 J from a hot reservoir and rejects 900 J to a cold reservoir. Its efficiency is:
What is the Reynolds number for water flowing at 1 m/s in a pipe of diameter 0.01 m? (η = 0.001 Pa·s, ρ = 1000 kg/m³)
A water jet exits a nozzle at 10 m/s and strikes a stationary wall normally. The mass flow rate is 0.05 kg/s. What is the force exerted on the wall?
details.explanation: Force = rate of change of momentum = ṁ × Δv = 0.05 × 10 = 0.5 N.

details.question: To break a wire, a stress of 10⁶ N/m² is required. If density = 3 × 10³ kg/m³, the length that will break under its own weight is:
details.explanation: At breaking: σ = ρgL → L = σ/(ρg) = 10⁶/(3000 × 9.8) ≈ 34 m

details.question: In the Joule-Thomson effect, a real gas expands through a porous plug. For most gases below the inversion temperature, the temperature:
details.explanation: Below the inversion temperature, the Joule-Thomson effect causes cooling during throttling (intermolecular attraction dominates). This is the basis of gas liquefaction.

details.question: A hydraulic press works on the principle of:
details.explanation: Pascal's law states that pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted equally in all directions — the operating principle of hydraulic presses and lifts.

details.question: Sound intensity level is measured in:
details.explanation: Sound intensity level L = 10 log₁₀(I/I₀) is measured in decibels (dB), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² is the threshold of hearing.

details.question: A wave has a period of 0.02 s. Its frequency is:
An object floats when:

options.a: Its density equals the liquid density
options.b: Its weight equals the buoyant force
options.c: Its density is less than the liquid density

details.explanation: An object floats when buoyant force = weight. This occurs when average density ≤ liquid density. Both statements (b) and (c) describe this condition.

details.question: A Carnot engine operates between 500 K and 300 K. Its maximum possible efficiency is:
The speed of sound increases with temperature because:

options.b: Molecular speed increases, increasing bulk modulus effectively
options.c: Wavelength increases while frequency stays constant
options.d: Sound becomes electromagnetic at higher temperatures

details.explanation: v = √(γRT/M). Speed of sound ∝ √T. Higher temperature → faster molecules → more rapid pressure propagation. v ≈ 331√(T/273) m/s.

details.question: The Boltzmann constant k_B relates:

options.b: Kinetic energy of a molecule to absolute temperature

details.explanation: k_B = R/N_A = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K. It relates the average translational KE per molecule (3k_BT/2) to absolute temperature T.

details.question: The hydrostatic pressure at a given depth in a liquid depends on:

options.c: Only the depth and density of the liquid
options.d: The total weight of liquid above

details.explanation: Hydrostatic paradox: P = ρgh depends only on depth h and density ρ, not on container shape or total volume. This is why dams must be strong at the base regardless of reservoir shape.

details.question: A spinning ball curves in flight due to the:
details.explanation: The Magnus effect: a spinning ball drags air around it, creating a pressure difference (Bernoulli) between top and bottom, resulting in a net force causing the ball to curve.

details.question: A liquid has volume expansivity β = 3.6 × 10⁻⁴ °C⁻¹. Its initial volume is 0.50 m³ and temperature rises by 40°C. What is the change in volume?
A floating body is in stable equilibrium when:

options.a: The center of gravity is above the metacenter
options.b: The metacenter is above the center of gravity
options.c: Center of gravity coincides with center of buoyancy

details.explanation: Stable floating equilibrium requires the metacenter M to be above the center of gravity G. When tilted, the righting moment restores the body to equilibrium.

details.question: A thermometer reads 20°C in ice water and 100°C in boiling water. What is the Fahrenheit value at 60°C?
details.explanation: The thermometer's range: 20°C to 100°C = 80 divisions. 60°C is 40 units above ice point. Fraction = 40/80 = 0.5. On Fahrenheit scale: F = 32 + 0.5 × 180 = 32 + 90 = ... but the selected answer is 140°F. Using the non-standard scale: fraction of scale = (60-20)/(100-20) = 0.5; mapping to F = 32 + 0.5×(212-32) = 32+90 = 122°F. The course selects c = 140°F — verify with your lecturer's notes.

details.question: Which material property describes resistance to scratching or indentation?
details.explanation: Hardness measures resistance to surface indentation/scratching (Mohs scale, Brinell, Rockwell, Vickers tests). Stiffness = resistance to elastic deformation. Toughness = energy absorbed before fracture.

details.question: A hydraulic lift has a small piston of area 0.02 m² and a large piston of area 0.4 m². If a force of 100 N is applied to the small piston, what is the corresponding force on the large piston?
details.explanation: By Pascal's Law: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂ → F₂ = F₁ × A₂/A₁ = 100 × 0.4/0.02 = 2000 N

details.question: Cavitation occurs in a fluid when:

options.b: Local pressure drops below vapor pressure causing bubble formation

details.explanation: Cavitation: when local pressure drops below vapor pressure, the liquid vaporizes forming bubbles. When bubbles collapse near surfaces they cause severe damage (ship propellers, pump impellers).

details.question: A tyre pressure is 22 kPa at 30°C. After a journey, pressure becomes 25 kPa at constant volume. What is the final temperature?
details.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law: P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂ → T₂ = T₁ × P₂/P₁ = 303 × 25/22 = 344.32 K = 71.32°C

details.question: Gauge pressure is:
details.explanation: Gauge pressure = Absolute pressure − Atmospheric pressure. It is the pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure.

details.question: The ratio of stress to strain within the elastic limit is called:
details.explanation: By Hooke's Law, stress/strain = Modulus of Elasticity (also called Young's Modulus for longitudinal stress).

details.question: The coefficient of performance (COP) of a refrigerator is defined as:
details.explanation: COP_refrigerator = Q_C/W, the heat removed from the cold reservoir divided by the work input. A higher COP means a more efficient refrigerator.

details.question: Evaluate the gauge pressure inside a soap bubble of radius 8.4 × 10⁻⁵ m given a surface tension of 0.038 N/m:
The rms speed of gas molecules is:
details.explanation: v_rms = √(3RT/M) = √(3k_BT/m). Note: v_avg = √(8RT/πM), v_mp = √(2RT/M). v_rms > v_avg > v_mp.

details.question: The quality factor Q of an oscillator describes:

options.b: The sharpness of resonance and rate of energy decay

details.explanation: Q = ω₀/Δω (resonant frequency / bandwidth). High Q: sharp resonance, slow energy decay (lightly damped). Low Q: broad resonance, rapid decay (heavily damped).

details.question: What is the entropy change when 2000 J of heat is reversibly added at 400 K?
The slope of the stress-strain curve within the elastic limit gives:
details.explanation: Young's modulus E = stress/strain = the slope of the linear (elastic) portion of the stress-strain curve.

details.question: A metal sphere of diameter 10 mm is dropped into glycerin. Viscous force = 2.5 N; Terminal velocity = 6 m/s. The coefficient of viscosity η is:
The pressure at a certain depth in a liquid column is given by:
details.explanation: Hydrostatic pressure: P = ρgh, where ρ is density, g is gravitational acceleration, and h is depth.

details.question: A Carnot engine operates between 627°C and 27°C. Its efficiency is:
Evaporation occurs:

options.b: At all temperatures from the surface of a liquid

details.explanation: Evaporation (surface phenomenon) occurs at any temperature as high-energy surface molecules escape. Boiling occurs throughout the liquid only at the boiling point.

details.question: For most engineering materials, Poisson's ratio lies in the range:
details.explanation: For stable isotropic materials, 0 < ν < 0.5. Rubber ≈ 0.49 (nearly incompressible), cork ≈ 0, steel ≈ 0.3. Negative ν is possible only in special auxetic materials.

details.question: The principle of calorimetry states:

options.a: Heat lost = Heat gained for mixed substances
options.b: Total energy is converted to heat
options.d: Heat capacity is constant for all materials

details.explanation: Principle of calorimetry (method of mixtures): heat lost by hot body = heat gained by cold body, when no heat is lost to surroundings.

details.question: Dancing of camphor on water is due to:
details.explanation: Camphor dissolves unevenly, lowering surface tension on one side. The imbalance in surface tension forces pulls the camphor across the water surface.

details.question: When a wave travels from a denser to a rarer medium, the reflected wave undergoes:
details.explanation: Reflection from rarer medium (lower impedance): no phase change. Reflection from denser medium (higher impedance): phase reversal of π. This applies to both mechanical and EM waves.

details.question: Which law states that at constant temperature, the volume of a given mass of gas is inversely proportional to its pressure?
details.explanation: Boyle's Law: PV = constant (at constant T and n). Volume ∝ 1/P.

details.question: An open glass tube is immersed in mercury so that 8 cm extends above the mercury level. The tube is sealed and raised vertically by 46 cm. (Atmospheric pressure = 76 cm of Hg). The length of the air column now is:
details.explanation: Initial: L₁ = 8 cm, P₁ = 76 cmHg. After raising 46 cm: tube top is 54 cm above original mercury, new air column length = L₂. New pressure P₂ = 76 − (54 − L₂) cmHg. By Boyle's law: P₁L₁ = P₂L₂ → 76×8 = (76−54+L₂)×L₂ → 608 = (22+L₂)L₂. Solving: L₂² + 22L₂ − 608 = 0 → L₂ = (−22 + √(484+2432))/2 = (−22+54)/2 = 16 cm.

details.question: A closed pipe (closed at one end) supports only:
details.explanation: A closed pipe has a node at the closed end and antinode at the open end. This allows only odd harmonics: f_n = nv/4L where n = 1, 3, 5, ...

details.question: A rod of cross-sectional area 0.01 m² supports a load of 5000 N. The stress in the rod is:
The speed of longitudinal waves in a solid rod is:
details.explanation: v = √(E/ρ) for longitudinal waves in a solid rod, where E is Young's modulus and ρ is density. For bulk waves in an extended solid, v = √((K + 4G/3)/ρ).

details.question: If the length of a wire is doubled, its Young's modulus:
details.explanation: Young's modulus is an intrinsic material property. It does not depend on the dimensions (length or cross-section) of the wire.

details.question: When a gas is compressed, work is done:
details.explanation: Compression: external agent (surroundings) does positive work ON the gas. W_on_gas > 0, so W_by_gas < 0. By First Law: ΔU = Q + W_on = Q − W_by.

details.question: Which equation represents the First Law of Thermodynamics?
details.explanation: The First Law of Thermodynamics states that heat added to a system equals the increase in internal energy plus the work done by the system: ΔQ = ΔU + W.

details.question: A wire of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 1×10⁻⁶ m² stretches by 0.002 m when a 100 N force is applied. Young's modulus is:
Specific gravity (relative density) of a substance is defined as:

options.b: Ratio of its density to density of water at 4°C

details.explanation: Specific gravity = ρ_substance/ρ_water(4°C). It is dimensionless. For water SG = 1; for mercury SG ≈ 13.6.

details.question: A capillary tube of radius 0.42 mm is dipped in a liquid (T = 0.085 N/m, θ = 32°, ρ = 1260 kg/m³). The height of rise is:
Capillary action is the result of:
details.explanation: Capillary action results from adhesion (liquid-to-wall attraction) and cohesion (liquid-to-liquid attraction), combined with surface tension.

details.question: The volume flow rate Q (m³/s) ∝ η^a × d^b × (dP/dx)^c. Find the values of a, b, and c:
details.explanation: From Poiseuille's law Q = πr⁴/(8η) × (ΔP/L), dimensional analysis gives Q ∝ η⁻¹ × d⁴ × (dP/dx)¹, so (a, b, c) = (−1, 4, 1).

details.question: The equation of continuity in fluid dynamics expresses:
details.explanation: The continuity equation A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (for incompressible flow) is a statement of conservation of mass: mass flow rate is constant.

details.question: A sphere of radius r falls through a viscous fluid with terminal velocity v. The viscous drag force is given by:
details.explanation: Stokes' Law: F = 6πηrv, where η is dynamic viscosity, r is sphere radius, and v is the velocity.

details.question: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature:
details.explanation: Boyle's Law: PV = constant (at constant temperature). As pressure increases, volume decreases proportionally.

details.question: A nurse added 2.2 × 10⁶ J of heat to 2 kg of water at 70°C. How much steam is produced? (L_v ≈ 2.5 × 10⁶ J/kg, c = 4200 J/kg·K)
Water leaks from a small hole at the base of a tank filled to height h = 5 m. The exit speed is: (g = 10 m/s²)
details.explanation: By Torricelli's theorem: v = √(2gh) = √(2 × 10 × 5) = √100 = 10 m/s

details.question: Fourier's law of heat conduction states that heat flow rate is proportional to:
details.explanation: Q/t = −kA(dT/dx). Heat flow rate is proportional to thermal conductivity k, cross-sectional area A, and the temperature gradient dT/dx.

details.question: As temperature increases, surface tension of a liquid:
details.explanation: Higher temperature increases molecular kinetic energy and reduces intermolecular cohesive forces, thereby decreasing surface tension. Surface tension → 0 at critical temperature.

details.question: Which of the following is the correct unit of Young's modulus?
details.explanation: Young's modulus = stress/strain = (N/m²)/dimensionless = Pa (Pascal).

details.question: Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that the total pressure of a gas mixture equals:
details.explanation: P_total = P₁ + P₂ + P₃ + ... Each gas exerts pressure independently as if it alone occupied the container.

details.question: Two drops of a liquid are merged to form a single drop. In this process:
details.explanation: When two drops merge, total surface area decreases. Since surface energy = T × A, a reduction in area means energy is released.

details.question: A 2 kg block on a horizontal frictionless surface is attached to a wall spring (k = 800 N/m) and oscillates. A 0.5 kg lump of clay is dropped vertically onto the block when the block passes through equilibrium at v = 4 m/s. What is the new amplitude?
A conical pendulum has a string of length 0.5 m and makes a half-angle of 30° with the vertical. What is the period of revolution? (g = 10 m/s², cos30° = 0.866)
A 2000 kg truck moving at 15 m/s hits a stationary 500 kg car. They lock together. What percentage of the initial KE is lost?
A particle of mass 3 kg is in a potential energy field U = 3x² − 12x + 9 J. Find the equilibrium position and determine if it is stable.
options: a. x = 2 m; stable (U has a minimum there)

details.question: A satellite orbits Earth at a radius of 2R_E. What is its orbital speed if the orbital speed at R_E is v₀?
A force of 300 N is applied to a wheel at radius 0.4 m. The wheel has I = 3 kg·m². What is the angular acceleration?
A uniform ladder of length 5 m and mass 20 kg leans against a frictionless wall at 60° to the horizontal. What is the normal reaction from the wall? (g = 10 m/s²)
A solid sphere of mass 2 kg rolls without slipping and has translational KE = 5 J. What is its total KE? (I = 2/5 MR²)
A 500 g ball is attached to a string of length 0.6 m and swings in a vertical circle. If the speed at the bottom is 3 m/s, what is the tension at the bottom? (g = 10 m/s²)

Using energy methods, a 3 kg block compresses a spring (k = 900 N/m) by 0.2 m. The spring fires the block up a frictionless 37° incline. How far along the incline does the block travel? (sin37° = 0.6, g = 10 m/s²)
A projectile is fired from the top of a cliff 80 m high at 20 m/s horizontally. What is the velocity just before impact? (g = 10 m/s²)
Two concurrent forces of 8 N and 6 N act on an object. The minimum possible resultant is:
What fraction of the kinetic energy is lost in a perfectly inelastic collision between equal masses?
A body of mass 2 kg executes SHM with amplitude 0.3 m and frequency 0.5 Hz. What is the maximum restoring force?
A ball is thrown at 37° above horizontal at 25 m/s. How far does it travel horizontally before hitting the ground? (sin37° = 0.6, cos37° = 0.8, g = 10 m/s²)
A projectile is fired at 45° with speed 20 m/s. What is its horizontal range? (g = 10 m/s²)
A body oscillating in SHM has the equation of motion a = −100x. What is the time period? (x in metres, a in m/s²)
options: c. Both a and b are the same

details.question: Two masses M and m are connected by a massless rod of length d. They rotate freely about the rod's centre of mass. What is the kinetic energy if their common angular velocity is ω?
In which of these situations is angular momentum about the centre conserved for a single particle moving on a curved path?
options: a. When the net torque about the centre is zero (central force)

details.question: If the kinetic energy of a body is doubled by increasing its speed, by what factor was the original speed increased?
A 500 g toy car is driven by a compressed spring (k = 80 N/m, compressed 0.1 m). On a horizontal surface (μk = 0.2), how far does the car travel from the release point? (g = 10 m/s²)
What is the minimum speed needed at the top of a loop-the-loop of radius 5 m to maintain contact? (g = 10 m/s²)
A projectile is launched from ground level. It travels 120 m horizontally and lands at the same height in 4 s. What was the launch angle? (g = 10 m/s²)
A particle moves along the x-axis such that x = t³ − 6t² + 9t + 2 m. At what time(s) is the particle momentarily at rest?
options: a. t = 1 s and t = 3 s
c. t = 0 s and t = 2 s

details.question: A projectile is launched at 30° with an initial speed of 40 m/s. What is the maximum height reached? (g = 10 m/s²)
Two identical masses m are connected by a light rod of negligible mass. One mass is at (0,0) and the other at (4,3) m. Where is the centre of mass?
A satellite at height h above Earth's surface has an orbital period T. If the satellite moves to height 4h, what is its new period? (R_E negligible compared to h)
A 200 g mass on a 0.5 m string describes a horizontal circle at 3 rad/s. What is the period of revolution?

A 2 kg ball is tied to a 1 m string and moves in a horizontal circle such that the string makes 60° with the vertical. If the string can handle a maximum tension of 40 N, what is the maximum speed? (g = 10 m/s²)
A 0.3 kg ball on a 0.8 m string moves in a horizontal circle at 5 m/s. What is the tension in the string?
A 0.2 kg mass attached to a spring (k = 80 N/m) is displaced 0.05 m from equilibrium and released. What is the maximum KE during oscillation?
An object undergoes projectile motion. Which component of velocity remains constant throughout?
A 3 kg particle is subject to a centripetal force F = 27 N in a circle of radius 3 m. What is the kinetic energy of the particle?
A force of 100 N is applied at the end of a 2 m lever arm. The perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action is 1.5 m. What is the torque?
A body is in equilibrium under the action of three concurrent forces. If one force is 10 N northward and another is 10 N eastward, the third force must be:
options: a. 10√2 N at 45° south of west

details.question: Two objects of mass 2 kg and 4 kg have momenta of equal magnitude but opposite direction. They collide and stick together. What is the common velocity?
options: b. The velocity of the 2 kg object
c. The velocity of the 4 kg object

details.question: A 4 kg and a 6 kg mass hang on either side of a pulley with a 1 kg pulley (I = ½MR², R = 0.1 m). What is the acceleration of the system? (g = 10 m/s²)
Using the parallel-axis theorem, the moment of inertia of a solid sphere (I_cm = 2/5 MR²) about a tangential axis is:
options: c. Both a and b are correct (they are equal)

details.question: A 3 kg mass hangs from the end of a 4 m uniform rod (mass 2 kg) hinged at the wall. A vertical wire supports the rod at 1 m from the hinge. What is the tension in the wire? (g = 10 m/s²)
The angular momentum of a planet about the Sun is constant (Kepler's second law). This is because:
options: a. The gravitational force is always directed toward the Sun (central force, zero torque)
d. The planet's speed is constant

details.question: A 2 kg object is projected with velocity 10 m/s at 30° above horizontal. At maximum height, what is the KE? (g = 10 m/s²)

A 5 kg block on a horizontal surface is connected by a spring (k = 200 N/m) to a fixed wall. The surface has μk = 0.15. The block is pulled 0.3 m from equilibrium and released. How far does it travel before first momentarily stopping? (g = 10 m/s²)
What is the binding energy (total mechanical energy) of a satellite of mass m orbiting at radius r from Earth's centre (mass M)?
A horizontal platform rotates at 2 rad/s. A 3 kg mass sits at 0.5 m from the centre. What additional torque is needed to maintain constant angular velocity when the mass is moved to 1 m?
options: a. 0 N·m (no torque needed, angular momentum changes)

details.question: A push of 80 N moves a 5 kg block up a 30° inclined plane. The coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.25 and the length of the plane is 20 m. Compute the work done by the resultant force acting on the block.
What is the height gained by a 2 kg ball thrown vertically upward at 20 m/s using the work-energy theorem? (g = 10 m/s²)
Two forces of 5 N and 12 N act at right angles. What is the magnitude of their resultant?
A ball of mass 0.4 kg is thrown upward at 15 m/s. What impulse does gravity impart during the first 2 s? (g = 10 m/s²)
A uniform sphere is released from rest on an inclined plane of angle θ. If it rolls without slipping, the linear acceleration is:
A 3 kg mass moves under the influence of a conservative force. Its potential energy is U = 4x² − 8x + 3 J. What is the equilibrium position, and what is the frequency of small oscillations?
A 4 kg object undergoes circular motion at 3 m/s in a 2 m radius circle. What is its angular momentum about the centre?
What is the gravitational field intensity at the surface of a planet of mass 6×10²⁴ kg and radius 6.4×10⁶ m? (G = 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²)
A 3 kg particle undergoes SHM: x = 0.4 cos(5t − π/3) m. What is the phase angle at t = 0, and what is the initial velocity?
A 5 kg mass oscillates in SHM with equation x = 0.3 sin(6t) m. What is the maximum restoring force?
A rope connects a 6 kg block on a 37° incline (μk = 0.25) to a 4 kg block hanging vertically over a frictionless pulley. What is the acceleration? (g = 10 m/s², sin37° = 0.6, cos37° = 0.8)

A uniform meter rule weighs 1 kg and is pivoted at the 60 cm mark. A 40 g weight is suspended from the 100 cm mark. At which mark on the ruler should a 30 g mass be placed in order to keep the ruler in equilibrium?
A 10 kg object is lifted by a pulley system with mechanical advantage 4. What effort is needed? (g = 10 m/s², ignore pulley mass)
What is the velocity of a body at the highest point of a projectile that was launched at 45° with speed 20 m/s?
The velocity of a particle is v = 2t² − 4t + 3 m/s. What is the average acceleration over the interval t = 1 s to t = 3 s?

A 5 kg block on a rough horizontal surface (μk = 0.2) is connected to a spring (k = 200 N/m). It is displaced 0.15 m and released. What is the maximum speed during the subsequent motion? (g = 10 m/s²)
Water flows through a pipe of cross-section 4 cm² at 3 m/s. If the pipe narrows to 2 cm², the new speed is:
details.explanation: By continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ → v₂ = A₁v₁/A₂ = (4 × 3)/2 = 6 m/s

details.question: Flow becomes turbulent when the Reynolds number exceeds approximately:
details.explanation: For flow in a pipe, laminar flow exists for Re < ~2000. Above ~4000 flow is turbulent. Between 2000–4000 is a transitional regime.

details.question: A brittle material:

options.a: Has a large plastic deformation region before fracture
options.b: Fractures with little or no plastic deformation

details.explanation: Brittle materials (e.g., glass, cast iron) fracture suddenly with little plastic deformation. Ductile materials (e.g., copper) show significant elongation before fracture.

details.question: The efficiency of a heat engine is 25%. If it absorbs 800 J of heat, how much work does it perform?
In an isothermal process for an ideal gas:
details.explanation: For an isothermal process (T = constant), the ideal gas law gives PV = nRT = constant. Hence PV = constant (Boyle's Law).

details.question: 37°C is equal to how many degrees Fahrenheit?
details.explanation: F = (9/5)C + 32 = (9/5×37) + 32 = 66.6 + 32 = 98.6°F. Note: The course may accept a = 98.0°F as an approximation — verify with your lecturer.

details.question: Two drops of a liquid are merged to form a single drop. In this process:
details.explanation: When two drops merge, total surface area decreases. Since surface energy = T × A, a reduction in area means energy is released.

details.question: Which property resists the flow of a fluid between layers moving at different velocities?
details.explanation: Viscosity is the internal friction of a fluid that resists relative motion between adjacent fluid layers. Surface tension acts at the liquid surface, not between internal layers.

details.question: A closed pipe of length 34 cm resonates at its fundamental frequency. The frequency is: (v_sound = 340 m/s)
The phase velocity of a wave is given by:
details.explanation: Phase velocity v_p = fλ = ω/k. Both expressions are equivalent (ω = 2πf, k = 2π/λ). Group velocity is v_g = dω/dk.

details.question: A molecule at the surface of a liquid experiences a net force:

options.b: Directed into the bulk of the liquid

details.explanation: Surface molecules are attracted by molecules below and to the sides but not from above (no liquid above). The net intermolecular force is directed inward into the bulk.

details.question: Bulk modulus is related to:
details.explanation: Bulk modulus K = −V(dP/dV) relates stress to volumetric strain (change in volume).

details.question: A hydraulic lift has a small piston of area 0.02 m² and a large piston of area 0.4 m². If a force of 100 N is applied to the small piston, what is the corresponding force on the large piston?
details.explanation: By Pascal's Law: F₁/A₁ = F₂/A₂ → F₂ = F₁ × A₂/A₁ = 100 × 0.4/0.02 = 2000 N

details.question: Capillary rise explains which phenomena?

options.a: The flow of blood in veins
options.b: The movement of water in porous paper
options.c: The absorption of water by roots

details.explanation: Capillary action explains water movement in narrow tubes/pores — it applies to blood flow in capillaries, chromatography paper, and water absorption in plant roots.

details.question: An isothermal process for an ideal gas satisfies PVⁿ = constant where n =
details.explanation: Polytropic process: PVⁿ = constant. n=0: isobaric; n=1: isothermal; n=γ: adiabatic; n=∞: isochoric.

details.question: Wave number k is defined as:
details.explanation: k = 2π/λ (rad/m). Wave number represents spatial frequency — cycles per unit length multiplied by 2π. Related to angular frequency: k = ω/v.

details.question: Specific gravity (relative density) of a substance is defined as:

options.b: Ratio of its density to density of water at 4°C

details.explanation: Specific gravity = ρ_substance/ρ_water(4°C). It is dimensionless. For water SG = 1; for mercury SG ≈ 13.6.

details.question: A capillary tube of radius 0.5 mm is dipped in water. The height of rise is: (T = 0.072 N/m, ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.8 m/s², θ ≈ 0°)
A structural bolt has tensile strength 400 MPa and factor of safety 4. The maximum working stress is:
details.explanation: Working stress = Tensile strength / Factor of safety = 400/4 = 100 MPa

details.question: Raindrops are spherical due to:
details.explanation: Surface tension acts to minimize the surface area of a liquid for a given volume. A sphere has the minimum surface area for a given volume, so free liquid droplets form spheres.

details.question: Poisson's ratio is defined as:
details.explanation: Poisson's ratio ν = −(lateral strain)/(longitudinal strain). It is always positive (material expands laterally when compressed longitudinally).

details.question: A force of 140 N stretches a horizontal spring by 0.25 m with a 0.7 kg block attached. Spring constant = 1200 N/m. If the system performs SHM, the maximum acceleration is:
details.explanation: Course method: a_max = F/m = 140/0.7 = 200 m/s². (Note: The standard SHM formula a_max = kA/m = 1200×0.25/0.7 ≈ 428 m/s², but the course uses F/m directly.)

details.question: The third harmonic of a fundamental frequency of 100 Hz is:
details.explanation: nth harmonic = n × fundamental frequency. Third harmonic = 3 × 100 = 300 Hz.

details.question: For an ideal gas, Cp − Cv equals:
details.explanation: For an ideal gas, Cp − Cv = R (universal gas constant). This is the Mayer relation, arising because extra work PΔV = nRT is done during constant pressure heating.

details.question: Calculate the average kinetic energy of one gas molecule at T = 300 K (k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K):
Water rises in a capillary tube because:
details.explanation: When adhesive forces (liquid–glass) exceed cohesive forces (liquid–liquid), the meniscus is concave and liquid rises. Mercury falls because cohesion > adhesion.

details.question: A soap bubble has diameter 4 mm (surface tension = 2.8 × 10⁻² N/m, atmospheric pressure = 1 × 10⁵ N/m²). Pressure inside the bubble is:
details.explanation: Gauge pressure inside soap bubble (2 surfaces): ΔP = 4T/r = 4 × 0.028/0.002 = 56 Pa. P_inside = 10⁵ + 56 ≈ 1.0056 × 10⁵ N/m²

details.question: The coefficient of linear expansion of iron is 1.0 × 10⁻⁵ per °C. An iron cube with edge length 5.0 cm is heated from 10°C to 60°C. By how much does the surface area increase?
details.explanation: Total surface area of cube = 6 × (5)² = 150 cm². ΔA = 2α × A × ΔT = 2 × 10⁻⁵ × 150 × 50 = 0.15 cm²

details.question: When heat is absorbed by a system and no work is done, the First Law becomes:
details.explanation: If W = 0: ΔQ = ΔU + W → ΔQ = ΔU → ΔU = Q. All absorbed heat goes into increasing internal energy.

details.question: The pressure of an ideal gas is increased by keeping temperature constant. The kinetic energy of the molecules:

options.d: Increases or decreases depending on the nature of gas

details.explanation: For an ideal gas, KE_avg = (3/2)kT — it depends only on temperature, not pressure. At constant temperature, KE remains unchanged.

details.question: In an adiabatic process with γ = 1.4, if pressure doubles during compression, the volume changes by a factor of:
One mole of an ideal gas at 300 K occupies 0.024 m³. Using R = 8.31 J/mol·K, calculate its pressure.
The excess pressure inside a soap bubble of radius R and surface tension T is:
details.explanation: A soap bubble has two surfaces. Excess pressure = 4T/R. For a single liquid drop or air bubble in liquid, excess pressure = 2T/R.

details.question: Work done by a gas in an isobaric (constant pressure) expansion from V₁ to V₂ is:
details.explanation: W = ∫PdV = P∫dV = P(V₂ − V₁) at constant pressure. For isothermal: W = nRTln(V₂/V₁).

details.question: The average kinetic energy of gas molecules is directly proportional to:
details.explanation: From kinetic theory: KE_avg = (3/2)kT, where T is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

details.question: The equation of continuity in fluid dynamics expresses:
details.explanation: The continuity equation A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ (for incompressible flow) is a statement of conservation of mass: mass flow rate is constant.

details.question: A good thermal conductor has:
details.explanation: Thermal conductivity k measures how readily heat flows through a material. Metals like copper and silver have high k values and are good thermal conductors.

details.question: Above the critical temperature:

options.a: A gas can always be liquefied by pressure
options.b: A gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone

details.explanation: Above the critical temperature T_c, no amount of pressure can liquefy a gas — the distinction between liquid and gas phases disappears. The substance exists as a supercritical fluid.

details.question: The heat given to a substance during a phase change is:
details.explanation: Latent heat is the heat absorbed or released during a phase change at constant temperature.

details.question: The slope of the stress-strain curve within the elastic limit gives:
details.explanation: Young's modulus E = stress/strain = the slope of the linear (elastic) portion of the stress-strain curve.

details.question: Evaluate the gauge pressure inside a soap bubble of radius 8.4 × 10⁻⁵ m given a surface tension of 0.038 N/m:
Which of the following is the correct unit of Young's modulus?
details.explanation: Young's modulus = stress/strain = (N/m²)/dimensionless = Pa (Pascal).

details.question: For a monatomic ideal gas, Cv equals:
details.explanation: A monatomic ideal gas has 3 translational degrees of freedom. By equipartition: Cv = (3/2)R.

details.question: The triple point of a substance is where:

options.a: All three states are in equilibrium simultaneously

details.explanation: At the triple point, specific temperature and pressure allow solid, liquid, and vapor to coexist in equilibrium. For water: T = 273.16 K, P = 611.73 Pa.

details.question: In steady flow, streamlines and pathlines:
details.explanation: In steady flow, the velocity at each point doesn't change with time. Therefore, every particle follows the same path, so streamlines and pathlines coincide.

details.question: Calculate the heat energy lost when 10 g of boiling water changes to ice at 0°C. (Specific Latent Heat of ice = 336 J/g; specific heat capacity of water = 4.2 J/g·K)
details.explanation: Heat lost = cooling from 100°C to 0°C + latent heat of freezing. Q = mcΔT + mL = 10×4.2×100 + 10×336 = 4200 + 3360 = 7560 J.

details.question: Gauge pressure is:
details.explanation: Gauge pressure = Absolute pressure − Atmospheric pressure. It is the pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure.

details.question: The ideal gas equation PV = nRT involves the universal gas constant R, which equals approximately:
details.explanation: R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) is the universal gas constant in PV = nRT. Note: k_B = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K is the Boltzmann constant (per molecule).

details.question: Normal human body temperature is 98.6°F (shown as 59.1°F in the question — likely a typo; using the standard value). What is it on the Kelvin scale?
Water has a very high specific heat capacity. This means water:

options.a: Heats up and cools down quickly
options.b: Stores large amounts of heat for small temperature changes

details.explanation: High specific heat (4200 J/kg·K) means water requires a lot of energy to change temperature. This makes it excellent for storing thermal energy and moderating climate.

details.question: For a cylindrical bubble (like a soap cylinder), the excess pressure inside is:
details.explanation: A cylindrical bubble has curvature in only one direction: ΔP = 2T/R (for a soap cylinder with 2 surfaces: ΔP = 4T/R? Actually for a long cylindrical soap film: pressure from one surface curvature = T/R each, total 2T/R). The standard result for a cylinder is 2T/R.

details.question: Water flows at 0.2 m³/s from a point 15 m above ground. (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²). Calculate the power that drives the water:
details.explanation: P = ρgQh = 1000×10×0.2×15 = 30,000 W. The course-selected answer is 20,000 W (corresponding to h=10 m). The exact value depends on the height used — verify with your lecturer.

details.question: On a stress-strain diagram, the region where stress is proportional to strain is called the:
details.explanation: The initial linear portion of the stress-strain curve is the proportional/elastic region where Hooke's Law applies. Beyond the proportional limit, the relationship becomes non-linear.

details.question: In SHM, velocity is maximum at:
details.explanation: v = ω√(A² − x²). At equilibrium (x = 0): v_max = ωA. At maximum displacement (x = A): v = 0.

details.question: Sound waves in air are:
details.explanation: Sound travels as longitudinal (compressional) waves — particles oscillate parallel to the direction of wave propagation through alternating compressions and rarefactions.

details.question: Cavitation occurs in a fluid when:

options.b: Local pressure drops below vapor pressure causing bubble formation

details.explanation: Cavitation: when local pressure drops below vapor pressure, the liquid vaporizes forming bubbles. When bubbles collapse near surfaces they cause severe damage (ship propellers, pump impellers).

details.question: A gas at 1.2 × 10⁵ Pa occupies 0.020 m³. It is compressed isothermally to 0.010 m³. What is the final pressure?
details.explanation: Boyle's Law: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ → P₂ = P₁V₁/V₂ = 1.2×10⁵ × 0.020/0.010 = 2.4 × 10⁵ Pa

details.question: The quality factor Q of an oscillator describes:

options.b: The sharpness of resonance and rate of energy decay

details.explanation: Q = ω₀/Δω (resonant frequency / bandwidth). High Q: sharp resonance, slow energy decay (lightly damped). Low Q: broad resonance, rapid decay (heavily damped).

details.question: Two springs with spring constants k₁ and k₂ are connected in series. The effective spring constant is:
details.explanation: Springs in series: 1/k_eff = 1/k₁ + 1/k₂ → k_eff = k₁k₂/(k₁ + k₂). The softer spring dominates.

details.question: A thermometer reads 20°C in ice water and 100°C in boiling water. What is the Fahrenheit value at 60°C?
details.explanation: The thermometer's range: 20°C to 100°C = 80 divisions. 60°C is 40 units above ice point. Fraction = 40/80 = 0.5. On Fahrenheit scale: F = 32 + 0.5 × 180 = 32 + 90 = ... but the selected answer is 140°F. Using the non-standard scale: fraction of scale = (60-20)/(100-20) = 0.5; mapping to F = 32 + 0.5×(212-32) = 32+90 = 122°F. The course selects c = 140°F — verify with your lecturer's notes.
